af_score_components.notes: MCP server with good quality (75). Exa is an AI-native web search API optimized for semantic search — different from traditional keyword search. Simple API key. Search results are structured with metadata — good for agent consumption. Filters for domain, date, content type. Highlights and summaries available. Rate limits vary significantly by tier — free tier restrictive for production agents. Neural search latency higher than keyword search.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. Simple API key — no scope granularity. Exa-maintained. No formal compliance certifications listed. API key via environment variable. Search queries may contain sensitive context — data retention policy should be reviewed for sensitive agent workflows. Read-only search operations minimize risk.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: SaaS with status page. Relatively stable API versioning. Migration guides available. Retry guidance documented. Full GET idempotency on search queries. Rate limits vary by tier — monitor for free tier exhaustion. Neural search latency varies with load. MCP server adds additional failure surface to base API reliability.

af_score_components.notes: Local MCP server for Excel file reading and writing (quality 72). No auth required — local file operations. Supports .xlsx, .xls formats. No rate limits (local tool). Reads/writes directly to file system. Cell-level addressing required — agents must know worksheet and cell coordinates. Large files can cause memory issues. Formulas are not evaluated — raw values only in most implementations. File locking conflicts if Excel has the file open simultaneously.

security_score_components.security_notes: Local file access with no network exposure. No credentials stored or required. File system permissions provide implicit access control. Unknown version and dependency audit status (likely uses openpyxl or xlrd). Macro-enabled files (.xlsm) may contain executable code — avoid opening untrusted Excel files. Read-only mode not enforced — agents can overwrite data.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Self-hosted local tool with no SLA. Version typically stable for core features. No documented breaking change policy. Read operations idempotent. Write operations can corrupt files if interrupted — no transactional safety. Excel format compatibility varies between .xls and .xlsx. File locking by Excel application prevents simultaneous access.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. Partner/enterprise API requiring signed agreement to access full documentation. API key + HMAC signature adds complexity for agents. Rate limits not publicly documented. Travel pricing is real-time — cache aggressively but invalidate properly. Search results are paginated with session tokens. Booking is irreversible without explicit cancellation — agents need confirmation flow. Availability changes between search and booking — must handle out-of-inventory errors.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. API key + HMAC signature required — both credentials must be protected. Limited scope granularity. Expedia maintained with enterprise infrastructure. API key and signing secret via environment variables. Partner agreement gates access, providing implicit authorization control. Payment/booking data requires PCI compliance in agent workflows.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Enterprise travel SaaS with SLA. Versioned API with migration guides. Retry guidance documented. Search operations idempotent. Booking operations not idempotent — always verify booking status before retrying. Availability data is real-time — stale cache leads to booking failures. Rate limits undocumented — contact support for production limits. Session tokens for multi-step booking flows expire.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. Good documentation for mobile CI/CD automation. Robot tokens are purpose-built for CI/CD agents — preferred over personal access tokens. Build triggers are async — agents must poll build status. Build metadata and artifacts accessible via GraphQL API. Rate limits documented by tier. App-signing credentials stored in EAS — agents don't need to manage certs directly. Build queues can be long on shared runners.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. Robot tokens for service accounts, personal access tokens for human users. Limited scope granularity on tokens. Expo-maintained with good security practices. Tokens via environment variables. EAS Secret management for app secrets prevents exposure in build logs. App signing credentials managed by EAS reduces key management burden for agents. No formal compliance certifications listed.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Managed SaaS with status page. Stable API versioning. Migration guides available. Retry guidance documented. Async build operations require polling — implement with appropriate timeouts. Build queue position unpredictable on free tier. Idempotent status queries safe to retry. Build cancellation available if stuck. Robot token expiry requires rotation handling.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. Clean API specifically for Expo-managed push notifications. Simple API key (optional for enhanced deliverability). Batch push notifications (up to 100 per request). Each notification in batch returns individual status — agent must check per-token results. Push tokens expire and become invalid — handle DeviceNotRegistered errors by removing tokens. APNs and FCM abstracted by Expo — no direct APNS/FCM key management needed.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. Optional API key — authenticated requests get priority delivery. No scope granularity. Expo-maintained. API key via environment variable. Push token management must not store sensitive user data alongside tokens. Expo abstracts APNs/FCM credentials — agents don't hold platform secrets directly. No formal compliance certifications listed.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: SaaS with status page. Stable API. Good backward compatibility. Retry guidance documented. Delivery not guaranteed — push is best-effort. Batch operations return per-token results — check each. DeviceNotRegistered and InvalidCredentials errors require token cleanup. Unauthenticated requests lower priority during high load — use API key for production. Push delivery depends on APNs/FCM uptime.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. AI model inference platform for image generation, video, audio, and ML models. Simple API key. Models identified by path string (e.g., 'fal-ai/flux/dev') — must know exact model IDs. Async queue for long-running models — agents must poll or use webhooks. Model-specific parameters vary widely — check per-model docs. Result URLs expire — download immediately after generation. GPU availability affects latency significantly.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. Simple API key — no scope granularity. fal.ai maintained. No formal compliance certifications listed for AI inference platform. API key via environment variable. Generated content (images, video) is stored temporarily on fal.ai servers — review data retention for sensitive workflows. Content moderation policies apply to generated outputs.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: SaaS with status page. Relatively stable API. Migration guides available. Retry guidance documented. Async queue required for GPU-heavy models — implement with timeouts. Result URLs expire after short TTL — must download immediately. GPU resource availability affects latency significantly. Model availability may change as fal.ai updates their model catalog. Rate limits per model vary.

af_score_components.notes: MCP server with moderate quality (55). Accesses the unofficial Fantasy Premier League API — no auth required for public team/player data. API is undocumented and unofficial — can change without notice when Premier League updates their platform. No rate limits published. Read-only access to FPL data: players, fixtures, standings, teams. Season-specific data — changes structure between seasons. Not suitable for production workflows requiring stable data contracts. Useful for sports analytics agents during active season.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS to FPL API. No credentials required or stored — minimal security surface. Read-only public data operations. Community-maintained with unknown dependency hygiene. Terms of service for FPL data use should be reviewed for commercial applications. No compliance certifications. Unofficial API access may violate FPL terms of service.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Unofficial API with no SLA — uptime matches FPL platform. API can change without notice between seasons or platform updates. No documented breaking change policy. Read operations idempotent. Data freshness depends on FPL platform update schedule (typically hourly during gameweeks). Off-season availability uncertain. Not suitable for production workflows requiring stable data contracts.

af_score_components.notes: MCP generation framework (quality 72) — auto-generates MCP server from FastAPI routes. Auth, rate limits, and error handling all inherit from the underlying FastAPI application. Tool descriptions auto-generated from OpenAPI spec — quality depends on route documentation quality. Requires running FastAPI app to function. Transforms existing REST APIs into MCP tools without rebuilding. Version tied to FastAPI ecosystem compatibility. Practical for teams with existing FastAPI services.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS if underlying FastAPI app uses HTTPS. Auth via FastAPI Depends — security depends entirely on how the FastAPI app is secured. Scope depends on FastAPI middleware and route-level auth. Dependency hygiene depends on FastAPI ecosystem. Credentials managed by FastAPI app. Security posture is inherited — properly secured FastAPI app yields properly secured MCP server, and vice versa.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Self-hosted (FastAPI app). Version tied to FastAPI compatibility. Breaking changes tied to FastAPI releases. Error recovery depends on underlying FastAPI app. Adds an additional abstraction layer between MCP client and FastAPI — debugging requires tracing through two layers. Auto-generated tool schemas may not perfectly reflect API semantics. Good solution for teams already invested in FastAPI.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. Excellent documentation with OpenAPI spec and multi-language SDK. Simple API key. Fastly is a CDN/edge platform — agents can manage services, purge cache, update VCL configs, read analytics. Config deploys are versioned — must activate version after update. Purge is irreversible — cache must be rebuilt from origin after purge. Service IDs required for all operations. Soft purge (mark stale) vs hard purge (delete) — important distinction for agents.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. API key with optional service-level restriction. Service-scoped tokens available for per-service isolation. SOC2/ISO27001/PCI-DSS compliance for CDN platform. API key via environment variable. Configuration management capabilities have high blast radius — wrong VCL can break all traffic. Read-only tokens available for monitoring-only workflows. Audit logging available.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Enterprise CDN SaaS with SLA and comprehensive status page. Stable versioned API. Good breaking change management. Retry guidance documented. Config versioning enables rollback. Cache purge idempotent (purging already-purged content is safe). Analytics API has eventual consistency — recent data may have delay. High traffic services benefit from staggered purge operations.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. Document database with FQL (Fauna Query Language) — requires learning proprietary query language. Simple API key auth. Serverless pricing model — costs scale with query complexity and reads/writes. Temporal data with document history built-in. Global distribution with strong consistency. FQL v10 has different syntax from v4 — migration required if using older version. Schema-optional but Collections and Documents require understanding of Fauna's data model.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. API key with role-based access control at collection and attribute level. Role-based permissions allow fine-grained data access control. Fauna maintained. API key via environment variable. Keys scoped to database — no cross-database access. Attribute-level security available for field-level access control. No formal compliance certifications publicly listed.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Managed serverless SaaS with status page. FQL v10 is stable versioned API. Migration guides for v4 to v10. Retry guidance documented. Optimistic concurrency control — transactions may abort under contention and require retry. Serverless pricing creates cost unpredictability for large agent workloads. Temporal reads are idempotent. Write transactions provide ACID guarantees — strong reliability for transactional workloads.
